bytes.topic: MLOps Philosophy — why reproducibility, versioning, and automation matter in ML
bytes.title: TensorRT MLOps — Your .engine Files Are Not Portable Artifacts
bytes.body:
TensorRT engines are device-specific compiled binaries. A build targeting an A100 (SM 8.0) will silently misbehave or outright fail on an L40S (SM 8.9). This makes versioning non-negotiable: track the full build fingerprint — ONNX model SHA-256, `trt.__version__`, CUDA version, and target SM — stored alongside every `.engine` file in your artifact registry.

Reproducibility requires more than version tracking. Without a timing cache (`ITimingCache`), TensorRT's tactic selection is non-deterministic across builds — the same ONNX can produce engines with measurably different latency profiles. Commit a per-GPU `timing.cache` to your artifact store; `config.set_timing_cache(cache, ignore_mismatch=False)` enforces SM consistency and locks tactic choices across rebuilds. For INT8 pipelines, also version the calibration cache — recalibrating on different data distributions silently shifts accuracy without any error.

Automate engine builds in CI per target GPU class. Gate every promotion with `polygraphy run model.onnx --trt --onnx` to validate output parity between the ONNX source and the compiled TRT engine. Engines that skip this gate are production landmines.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorrt as trt, hashlib, os

def engine_fingerprint(onnx_path: str) -> dict:
    sha = hashlib.sha256(open(onnx_path, "rb").read()).hexdigest()
    return {"onnx_sha256": sha, "trt_version": trt.__version__}

def build_with_timing_cache(builder, config, network, cache_path: str):
    blob = open(cache_path, "rb").read() if os.path.exists(cache_path) else b""
    cache = config.create_timing_cache(blob)
    config.set_timing_cache(cache, ignore_mismatch=False)  # hard-fail on SM mismatch
    engine = builder.build_serialized_network(network, config)
    open(cache_path, "wb").write(cache.serialize())
    return engine

bytes.topic: Experiment Tracking — logging metrics, params, artifacts, comparing runs
bytes.title: TensorRT Experiment Tracking — Versioning Engines, Params, and Latency Across Precision Runs
bytes.body:
TensorRT experiment tracking means more than logging loss curves — you're versioning engine build decisions that directly impact inference latency and throughput. Key params to capture per run: precision flags (`FP16`, `INT8`, `FP32`), `set_memory_pool_limit` workspace allocation, `optimization_level` (0–5), and dynamic shape profile bounds (min/opt/max per input tensor).

Log build time as a metric — it correlates tightly with `optimization_level` and spikes unexpectedly with large workspace limits. For INT8 runs, log the calibration algorithm (`IInt8EntropyCalibrator2` vs `IInt8MinMaxCalibrator`) and calibration dataset size, since these govern the accuracy-speed tradeoff.

Serialize the built engine via `builder.build_serialized_network()` and log it as an artifact alongside its calibration cache. Given the same CUDA version, driver, and GPU SKU, the same `.trt` file produces bit-identical inference — making runs truly reproducible.

Use MLflow's run comparison view to diff FP16 vs INT8 across builds. When you co-log a shared `eval_top1` accuracy metric with engine artifacts, the latency-accuracy frontier becomes immediately visible, letting you pick the right precision regime for your deployment SLA.
bytes.code_snippet:
import mlflow, tensorrt as trt, time

with mlflow.start_run():
    mlflow.log_params({"precision": "fp16", "workspace_gb": 4, "opt_level": 3})

    config = builder.create_builder_config()
    config.set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.FP16)
    config.set_memory_pool_limit(trt.MemoryPoolType.WORKSPACE, 4 << 30)
    config.builder_optimization_level = 3

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    serialized = builder.build_serialized_network(network, config)
    mlflow.log_metric("build_time_s", round(time.perf_counter() - t0, 3))

    with open("model.trt", "wb") as f:
        f.write(serialized)
    mlflow.log_artifact("model.trt")
    mlflow.log_metrics({"p50_latency_ms": 4.2, "throughput_fps": 238, "eval_top1": 0.761})

bytes.topic: MLflow — tracking server, model registry, projects, model serving
bytes.title: TensorRT + MLflow — Tracking Engine Builds, Registering Plans, and Serving Optimized Models
bytes.body: TensorRT engines are platform-specific serialized `.plan` files — MLflow's model registry is the right place to version and distribute them. When building an engine, log your `BuilderConfig` hyperparameters as MLflow params: precision (`FP16`, `INT8`), `max_workspace_size`, calibration dataset hash, and CUDA compute capability. Log engine size and throughput benchmarks as metrics. Serialize the engine with `engine.serialize()` and log it as an artifact via `mlflow.log_artifact('model.plan')`. For serving, wrap TensorRT inference in a `mlflow.pyfunc` custom model: implement `load_context` to deserialize the plan with `runtime.deserialize_cuda_engine()` and `predict` to run inference via a persistent `IExecutionContext`. This pattern lets you A/B compare FP32 vs FP16 engines across the same ONNX source model, trace latency regressions per build, and deploy via MLflow's REST scoring server without modifying your inference wrapper. Use MLflow Projects to pin the ONNX export + `trtexec` conversion pipeline so engine builds are fully reproducible across driver versions.
bytes.code_snippet:
import mlflow
import mlflow.pyfunc
import tensorrt as trt
import numpy as np

class TRTModel(mlflow.pyfunc.PythonModel):
    def load_context(self, context):
        runtime = trt.Runtime(trt.Logger(trt.Logger.WARNING))
        with open(context.artifacts["engine_path"], "rb") as f:
            self.engine = runtime.deserialize_cuda_engine(f.read())
        self.context = self.engine.create_execution_context()

    def predict(self, context, model_input):
        # bind inputs/outputs and run inference
        bindings = []
        for i in range(self.engine.num_bindings):
            shape = self.engine.get_binding_shape(i)
            dtype = trt.nptype(self.engine.get_binding_dtype(i))
            mem = np.ascontiguousarray(model_input.values if i == 0 else np.empty(shape, dtype=dtype))
            import pycuda.driver as cuda
            bindings.append(int(cuda.mem_alloc(mem.nbytes)))
        self.context.execute_v2(bindings=bindings)
        return bindings

with mlflow.start_run():
    mlflow.log_params({"precision": "FP16", "workspace_mb": 4096, "compute_cap": "8.6"})
    mlflow.log_artifact("model.plan", artifact_path="engine")
    mlflow.pyfunc.log_model("trt_model", python_model=TRTModel(),
        artifacts={"engine_path": "model.plan"})
    mlflow.register_model("runs:/<run_id>/trt_model", "ResNet50-TRT-FP16")

bytes.topic: Weights & Biases — experiment tracking, sweeps for hyperparameter search, reports
bytes.title: TensorRT + W&B — Tracking INT8 Calibration Quality and Engine Build Sweeps
bytes.body:
When optimizing a TensorRT engine, the INT8 calibration strategy and precision mode dramatically affect both accuracy and latency — but without systematic tracking, you're flying blind across engine builds. W&B lets you log calibration metrics, `trt.Builder` config flags, and post-build inference benchmarks as a unified experiment.

For each engine build, log the `IBuilderConfig` decisions — `max_workspace_size`, `set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.INT8)`, calibration algorithm (entropy vs. minmax), and calibration batch count. After building, run a latency benchmark loop and log throughput (samples/sec) and accuracy drop vs. FP32 baseline.

W&B Sweeps shine here: parameterize calibration batch size, workspace size, and precision mode, then let the sweep agent grid-search across builds. Each run logs its own serialized `.engine` artifact, so you can trace exactly which calibration config produced a given engine.

Reports are ideal for comparing INT8 accuracy degradation across model versions — plot mAP or top-1 accuracy against inference latency to find your Pareto-optimal TensorRT config before shipping.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorrt as trt
import wandb

wandb.init(project="trt-int8-sweeps", config={
    "precision": "INT8",
    "calib_batches": wandb.config.calib_batches,
    "max_workspace_gb": wandb.config.max_workspace_gb,
    "calib_algo": "entropy_v2",
})

builder = trt.Builder(trt.Logger(trt.Logger.WARNING))
config = builder.create_builder_config()
config.set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.INT8)
config.max_workspace_size = wandb.config.max_workspace_gb * (1 << 30)

# ... attach calibrator, build engine ...

wandb.log({
    "engine_size_mb": engine_size / 1e6,
    "p99_latency_ms": p99_latency,
    "throughput_samples_per_sec": throughput,
    "top1_accuracy": top1_acc,
    "fp32_accuracy_delta": fp32_acc - top1_acc,
})
wandb.save("model.engine")

bytes.topic: DVC — data versioning, pipeline stages, remote storage, reproducible pipelines
bytes.title: TensorRT + DVC — Versioning Engines, Calibration Data, and Reproducible Build Pipelines
bytes.body:
TensorRT engine files (.engine) are platform-specific artifacts — built against a fixed GPU architecture, CUDA version, and TRT version. Without versioning, a calibration dataset change silently invalidates your INT8 engine and you won't know until accuracy regresses in prod.

DVC solves this by treating your ONNX checkpoint, INT8 calibration cache, and serialized .engine as first-class versioned artifacts. Define a pipeline in dvc.yaml with three stages: export (PyTorch → ONNX), calibrate (generate calibration.cache via IInt8EntropyCalibrator2), and build (trtexec or Python API → engine.trt). Each stage hashes its inputs — if the ONNX graph or calibration set changes, only downstream stages re-run.

Store large .engine files on a DVC remote (S3, Azure Blob, GCS) so CI pulls the exact engine for a given commit without rebuilding from scratch on every runner. Tag experiments with dvc exp run --set-param precision=fp16 to compare FP16 vs INT8 latency reproducibly across GPU generations — critical when validating on A100 vs T4 before fleet rollout.
bytes.code_snippet:
# dvc.yaml
stages:
  export_onnx:
    cmd: python export.py --input model.pt --output model.onnx --opset 17
    deps: [model.pt, export.py]
    outs: [model.onnx]

  calibrate:
    cmd: python calibrate.py --data calib_data/ --cache calibration.cache
    deps: [model.onnx, calib_data/]
    outs: [calibration.cache]

  build_engine:
    cmd: >-
      trtexec --onnx=model.onnx
        --int8 --calib=calibration.cache
        --saveEngine=engine.trt
        --workspace=4096
    deps: [model.onnx, calibration.cache]
    outs: [engine.trt]

bytes.topic: Model Registry — versioning models, staging vs production, approval workflows
bytes.title: TensorRT Model Registry — Versioning .engine Files from Staging to Production
bytes.body:
TensorRT engines are not portable artifacts — a `.engine` file serialized on an A100 (sm_80) will silently fail or crash on an H100 (sm_90). Your registry must treat GPU architecture, TRT version, and CUDA version as first-class metadata, not optional annotations.

MLflow is the pragmatic choice here. Log the serialized engine via `mlflow.log_artifact()`, then tag each `ModelVersion` with `gpu_arch`, `trt_version`, and `precision` (fp16/int8). Staging means the engine has passed latency and accuracy benchmarks on a representative GPU; production means it has been validated on the exact target hardware SKU.

Approval workflow: block `transition_model_version_stage(..., stage='Production')` behind a CI gate that spins up a Triton Inference Server instance, loads the engine into a versioned model repository directory (e.g. `models/resnet50/2/model.plan`), and runs a smoke benchmark with `perf_analyzer`. Only on green does the MLflow stage transition fire. Never promote an engine that was built on a different `sm_` target than your serving fleet.
bytes.code_snippet:
import mlflow
import tensorrt as trt

def register_trt_engine(engine_path: str, gpu_arch: str) -> str:
    with mlflow.start_run() as run:
        mlflow.set_tags({
            "gpu_arch": gpu_arch,           # e.g. "sm_89"
            "trt_version": trt.__version__,  # e.g. "8.6.1"
            "precision": "fp16",
        })
        mlflow.log_artifact(engine_path, artifact_path="trt_engine")

    client = mlflow.tracking.MlflowClient()
    mv = client.create_model_version(
        name="resnet50-trt",
        source=f"runs:/{run.info.run_id}/trt_engine",
        run_id=run.info.run_id,
    )
    client.transition_model_version_stage(
        name="resnet50-trt", version=mv.version, stage="Staging"
    )
    return mv.version

bytes.topic: ONNX — cross-framework model format, export process, runtime inference
bytes.title: TensorRT + ONNX — Parsing and Building Optimized Engines from Cross-Framework Models
bytes.body:
ONNX acts as the universal IR between training frameworks and TensorRT's optimizer. The pipeline: export your model to ONNX, then parse it with `trt.OnnxParser` to populate an `INetworkDefinition`, which the `IBuilder` compiles into a serialized `.engine` file with fused kernels and precision calibration baked in.

When exporting from PyTorch, set `opset_version=17` and use `dynamic_axes` to avoid baking fixed batch dimensions — TensorRT's optimization profiles (`IOptimizationProfile`) need min/opt/max shape hints at build time to handle variable inputs efficiently. Skip `torch.onnx.export`'s `training=torch.onnx.TrainingMode.EVAL` and you'll serialize dropout/BN in training mode, silently tanking accuracy.

After parsing, call `builder.build_serialized_network(network, config)` with `config.set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.FP16)` or `INT8` (requires an `IInt8Calibrator`). The resulting blob is framework-agnostic — load it with `trt.Runtime.deserialize_cuda_engine()` on any TensorRT-compatible device. Engine files are not portable across TensorRT major versions or GPU architectures; rebuild when upgrading.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorrt as trt

TRT_LOGGER = trt.Logger(trt.Logger.WARNING)

def build_engine(onnx_path: str, fp16: bool = True) -> trt.ICudaEngine:
    builder = trt.Builder(TRT_LOGGER)
    network = builder.create_network(1 << int(trt.NetworkDefinitionCreationFlag.EXPLICIT_BATCH))
    parser = trt.OnnxParser(network, TRT_LOGGER)
    config = builder.create_builder_config()
    config.set_memory_pool_limit(trt.MemoryPoolType.WORKSPACE, 1 << 30)  # 1 GiB
    if fp16 and builder.platform_has_fast_fp16:
        config.set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.FP16)

    with open(onnx_path, "rb") as f:
        if not parser.parse(f.read()):
            for i in range(parser.num_errors):
                print(parser.get_error(i))
            raise RuntimeError("ONNX parse failed")

    profile = builder.create_optimization_profile()
    profile.set_shape("input", (1, 3, 224, 224), (8, 3, 224, 224), (32, 3, 224, 224))
    config.add_optimization_profile(profile)
    return builder.build_serialized_network(network, config)

bytes.topic: TensorRT — NVIDIA optimization, quantization, engine building, latency vs accuracy
bytes.title: TensorRT INT8 Calibration — Squeezing Latency Without Destroying Accuracy
bytes.body:
TensorRT's INT8 quantization reduces model weights and activations from FP32/FP16 to 8-bit integers, cutting inference latency by 2–4x on Ampere/Ada GPUs. The key is calibration: TensorRT profiles activation ranges on a representative dataset to compute per-tensor scale factors, minimizing the KL-divergence between FP32 and INT8 distributions.

Use `IInt8EntropyCalibrator2` for most vision models — it's more robust than MinMax for long-tailed distributions. Feed at least 500 representative samples; fewer causes poor scale estimates and accuracy collapse. Set `builder_config.set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.INT8)` and attach your calibrator before calling `builder.build_serialized_network()`.

For mixed precision, use `network.get_layer(i).precision = trt.DataType.FLOAT` to pin sensitive layers (e.g., detection heads, softmax) back to FP32. This preserves top-1 accuracy within 0.5% of baseline while retaining most latency gains.

Always benchmark with `trtexec --iterations=1000 --warmUp=500` — cold-start numbers are meaningless. Profile with Nsight Systems to identify any INT8 fallbacks that silently degrade throughput.

bytes.topic: Ray — distributed training, Ray Tune for HPO, Ray Serve for model serving
bytes.title: TensorRT + Ray Serve — Scaling Engine Replicas Without CUDA Context Conflicts
bytes.body:
TensorRT engines are serialized, device-specific binaries — they cannot be shared across processes without re-deserializing per CUDA context. Ray Serve's actor-based deployment model handles this correctly: each replica independently calls `trt.Runtime().deserialize_cuda_engine()`, creating an isolated `IExecutionContext` per GPU. Set `num_gpus: 1` in `ray_actor_options` to guarantee exclusive GPU allocation, preventing context contention that causes silent inference failures when two contexts share a device without explicit stream pinning.

For Ray Tune HPO, target TensorRT-specific knobs: `BuilderConfig.max_workspace_size`, INT8 calibration batch count, and dynamic shape profile bounds via `set_shape`. Tune against P99 latency measured through `trt.IProfiler` rather than accuracy alone — INT8 quantization can hide regressions that only surface under production batch distributions.

Engine versioning is clean with Ray Serve: assign a distinct `route_prefix` per `.engine` file in `serve.run()`, enabling zero-downtime swaps without tearing down the cluster or invalidating warm GPU contexts. Pair with Ray's placement groups to co-locate preprocessing actors and TensorRT inference replicas on the same node, eliminating PCIe transfer overhead between pipeline stages.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorrt as trt
from ray import serve

@serve.deployment(num_replicas=2, ray_actor_options={"num_gpus": 1})
class TRTEngineReplica:
    def __init__(self, engine_path: str):
        logger = trt.Logger(trt.Logger.WARNING)
        runtime = trt.Runtime(logger)
        with open(engine_path, "rb") as f:
            self.engine = runtime.deserialize_cuda_engine(f.read())
        self.context = self.engine.create_execution_context()

    async def __call__(self, request):
        # bind inputs: self.context.set_tensor_address(name, device_ptr)
        return {"num_io_tensors": self.engine.num_io_tensors}

serve.run(TRTEngineReplica.bind("/models/resnet50_int8.engine"), route_prefix="/v1/infer")

bytes.topic: Hyperparameter Optimization — grid vs random vs Bayesian search, early stopping
bytes.title: TensorRT Hyperparameter Search — Why Bayesian Beats Grid When Every Build Costs Minutes
bytes.body:
TensorRT's engine build config is its own hyperparameter space: workspace allocation (`MemoryPoolType.WORKSPACE`), precision flags (`BuilderFlag.FP16`, `BuilderFlag.INT8`), and dynamic shape profiles interact non-linearly with inference latency. Grid search is prohibitive — a 5×2×4 sweep across workspace sizes, precision modes, and batch profiles means 40 full engine builds, each taking several minutes on large models.

Random search improves coverage but wastes trials on already-explored regions. Bayesian search via Optuna's TPE sampler learns the latency surface from each completed build and steers subsequent trials toward promising configs — typically converging in 15–20 trials versus 30+ for random.

The TensorRT-specific accelerant: share a `ITimingCache` across all trials. Kernel tactic profiling dominates build time. Serializing the cache after each trial and reloading it in the next cuts redundant per-layer profiling by 60–80% on repeated architectures. For early stopping, Optuna's `MedianPruner` abandons trials whose intermediate latency already exceeds the running median — avoiding the full benchmark penalty on clearly inferior configs.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorrt as trt, optuna, pathlib
CACHE = pathlib.Path("tactic_cache.bin")
TRT_LOGGER = trt.Logger(trt.Logger.WARNING)

def objective(trial):
    cfg = trt.Builder(TRT_LOGGER).create_builder_config()
    cfg.set_memory_pool_limit(trt.MemoryPoolType.WORKSPACE,
                              trial.suggest_int("ws_mb", 512, 4096, step=512) << 20)
    if trial.suggest_categorical("fp16", [True, False]):
        cfg.set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.FP16)
    tc = cfg.create_timing_cache(CACHE.read_bytes() if CACHE.exists() else b"")
    cfg.set_timing_cache(tc, ignore_mismatch=False)
    latency = build_and_time(cfg)  # build engine + benchmark; serialize cache after
    CACHE.write_bytes(tc.serialize())
    return latency

optuna.create_study(direction="minimize", sampler=optuna.samplers.TPESampler(),
    pruner=optuna.pruners.MedianPruner(n_startup_trials=5)).optimize(objective, n_trials=25)

bytes.topic: Model Serving — REST APIs for inference, batching, latency SLAs
bytes.title: TensorRT + Triton — Enforcing Latency SLAs with Dynamic Batching
bytes.body:
When serving a TensorRT `.plan` file behind a REST API, Triton Inference Server is the standard path. The critical lever for latency SLAs is `max_queue_delay_microseconds` inside the `dynamic_batching` block of your `config.pbtxt`. This tells Triton the maximum time it will hold requests to form a larger batch before dispatching — set it too high and tail latency explodes; too low and you waste GPU throughput on batch-size-1 calls.

For a p99 latency SLA of 50 ms, a starting budget of 5,000–10,000 µs for the queue delay is reasonable, leaving the bulk of the budget for TRT engine execution. Pair this with `instance_group` set to `KIND_GPU` with `count: 2` to allow two concurrent `IExecutionContext` instances per GPU, overlapping H2D/D2H transfers with compute via separate CUDA streams.

Use `tritonclient.http` with `async_stream_infer` on the client side to pipeline requests without blocking on round-trips. Profile with `perf_analyzer -m your_model --concurrency-range 1:16` to find the concurrency level where throughput plateaus before your SLA breaks.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tritonclient.http as httpclient
import numpy as np

client = httpclient.InferenceServerClient(url="localhost:8000")

inputs = [httpclient.InferInput("input", [1, 3, 224, 224], "FP16")]
inputs[0].set_data_from_numpy(np.random.rand(1, 3, 224, 224).astype(np.float16))

outputs = [httpclient.InferRequestedOutput("output", binary_data=True)]

result = client.infer(
    model_name="resnet50_trt",
    inputs=inputs,
    outputs=outputs,
    headers={"Inference-Header-Content-Length": None},
)
print(result.as_numpy("output").shape)

bytes.topic: Model Monitoring — prediction drift, data drift, performance degradation in prod
bytes.title: TensorRT in Prod — Detecting Drift and Performance Degradation with IProfiler
bytes.body: TensorRT engines are compiled artifacts — they don't retrain or adapt. That makes drift invisible unless you explicitly instrument your inference pipeline. For prediction drift, snapshot output tensor statistics after each `enqueueV3()` call: compute running mean and std over a sliding window, then flag when the Z-score of the current batch mean exceeds a threshold. For data drift, validate input tensor distributions before `setTensorAddress()` — specifically watch for values outside the min/opt/max shape ranges defined in your `IOptimizationProfile`, which cause silent fallback to slower kernels. For performance degradation, implement `trt.IProfiler` to get per-layer execution times. This is the only way to distinguish GPU thermal throttling from a kernel regression introduced by a driver update or new engine rebuild. Pair the profiler output with `cudaEventElapsedTime` to get wall-clock latency independent of TRT's internal accounting. Persist baselines per engine version (use `ICudaEngine::getName()` as the key) so drift comparisons survive redeployments.

bytes.topic: CI/CD for ML — automated retraining, evaluation gates, deployment pipelines
bytes.title: TensorRT CI/CD — Engine Rebuild Gates, Polygraphy Accuracy Checks, and Safe Deployment
bytes.body:
TensorRT engines are GPU-architecture-specific — a rebuild is mandatory whenever the base model changes, CUDA compute capability shifts, or TensorRT version bumps. CI/CD pipelines must treat `.engine` artifacts as derived outputs, never as source artifacts.

A robust pipeline enforces three sequential gates:

1. **Build gate:** Convert the updated ONNX checkpoint to a TensorRT engine via `trtexec` with pinned `--optShapes`, `--fp16`, and `--workspace`. Commit these flags to your pipeline config to guarantee reproducibility across CI runners.

2. **Accuracy gate:** Run `polygraphy run <model.onnx> --trt --onnxrt --atol=1e-2 --rtol=1e-2` to diff TRT outputs against the ONNX reference. Numerical divergence is common after layer-fusion or INT8 calibration — catch it here, not in production.

3. **Latency gate:** Replay `trtexec --loadEngine --percentile=99 --iterations=500` and assert the p99 GPU compute time stays within SLA. Gate on p99, not mean — outliers kill production tail latency.

Store versioned `.engine` files in artifact storage tagged with TRT version, CUDA compute capability, and git SHA. Never deploy an engine built on a different GPU class than your production fleet.

bytes.topic: Feature Stores — centralized feature management, online vs offline features
bytes.title: TensorRT Feature Stores — Matching Online/Offline Feature Dtypes to Engine Bindings
bytes.body:
TensorRT engines are compiled with fixed input dtypes and shapes — FP16, INT8, or FP32 baked in at `trt.Builder` time. Feature stores (Feast, Tecton, Hopsworks) serve features as float64 or int64 by default, regardless of your model's precision. That mismatch silently corrupts inference: `cuda.memcpy_htod_async` will copy the wrong bytes to device memory with no exception, just wrong predictions.

For online features (low-latency, Redis-backed), pull the feature vector and immediately cast to match `engine.get_binding_dtype(binding_idx)` before the device copy. For FP16 engines this means `.astype(np.float16)` before touching the CUDA buffer.

For offline features (Parquet/columnar batch jobs), cast the entire DataFrame column before converting to a contiguous NumPy array, then run `context.execute_v2(bindings)` in chunks matching your `max_batch_size` set at build time. Misaligned batch sizes here cause a silent shape mismatch that TRT won't surface as an error in all versions.

Wrap your `FeatureStore.get_online_features()` call in a thin adapter that introspects `engine.get_binding_dtype()` and returns a dtype-correct, C-contiguous array. Centralizes the alignment logic instead of scattering `.astype()` calls across every inference site.
bytes.code_snippet:
import numpy as np
import tensorrt as trt
import pycuda.driver as cuda
from feast import FeatureStore

store = FeatureStore(repo_path=".")
fv = store.get_online_features(
    features=["user_embeddings:v1"],
    entity_rows=[{"user_id": uid}]
).to_dict()

raw = np.array(fv["user_embeddings__v1"], dtype=np.float32)

# Cast to match compiled TRT binding dtype
trt_dtype = engine.get_binding_dtype(0)
if trt_dtype == trt.DataType.HALF:
    raw = raw.astype(np.float16)
elif trt_dtype == trt.DataType.INT8:
    raw = raw.astype(np.int8)

cuda.memcpy_htod_async(d_input, np.ascontiguousarray(raw), stream)
context.execute_async_v3(stream_handle=stream.handle)
stream.synchronize()

bytes.topic: Quantization & Pruning — reducing model size, int8/fp16, trade-offs
bytes.title: TensorRT INT8 Calibration — Precision Trade-offs You Need to Know
bytes.body: TensorRT's INT8 mode can cut inference latency 2–4× over FP32, but naive activation quantizes poorly — you must supply a calibration dataset via IInt8EntropyCalibrator2 so TensorRT computes per-tensor dynamic ranges that minimize KL divergence. FP16 is safer: just set the BuilderFlag and TensorRT auto-selects which layers benefit; accuracy loss is typically <0.5% on modern Ampere/Ada GPUs with native FP16 tensor cores. INT8 requires 500–1000 representative batches through your calibrator, and the resulting calibration cache should be versioned alongside your engine — different GPU architectures produce different scales. Watch for layers TensorRT silently falls back to FP32 (e.g., SoftMax, certain normalizations); use trt.ILayer.precision and output_type to inspect or force precision per-layer. For structured sparsity (2:4 pattern), enable SPARSE_WEIGHTS — Ampere GPUs get near-2× speedup with zero calibration overhead, but you must pre-prune weights in PyTorch using torch.nn.utils.prune before export. Combining INT8 + sparsity rarely stacks perfectly; benchmark both paths with trtexec before committing.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorrt as trt

logger = trt.Logger(trt.Logger.WARNING)
builder = trt.Builder(logger)
config = builder.create_builder_config()
config.set_memory_pool_limit(trt.MemoryPoolType.WORKSPACE, 1 << 30)

# Enable FP16 (safe default)
config.set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.FP16)

# Enable INT8 + attach calibrator
config.set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.INT8)
config.int8_calibrator = MyEntropyCalibrator2(
    data_loader=calib_loader,
    cache_file="calib_cache.bin"
)

# Enable 2:4 structured sparsity (Ampere+)
config.set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.SPARSE_WEIGHTS)

bytes.topic: A/B Testing Models — shadow deployment, canary releases, champion/challenger
bytes.title: TensorRT A/B Testing — Shadow Deployment, Canary Releases, and Champion/Challenger
bytes.body:
Running multiple TensorRT engines simultaneously is the foundation of production model experimentation. In a shadow deployment, you serialize both engines via `trt.Builder` with identical `INetworkDefinition` inputs, execute the challenger engine in parallel using a separate `IExecutionContext`, but only return the champion's output. Compare logits or latency offline without affecting users.

For canary releases, partition traffic by request ID hash: route 5% to the challenger `IExecutionContext` and 95% to the champion, both sharing the same `ICudaEngine` loaded from distinct `.plan` files. Use `cudaStreamCreate()` per context to prevent stream contention — critical when both engines target the same GPU.

Champion/challenger promotion hinges on metrics: measure P99 latency via `cudaEventElapsedTime()` and track accuracy deltas against a labeled holdout set. Once the challenger exceeds the champion on both dimensions for a statistically significant window, swap the `.plan` file path in your engine loader and reload via `runtime->deserializeCudaEngine()` without downtime. Always version `.plan` files by `trtexec` build hash to ensure reproducibility across engine swaps.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorrt as trt
import pycuda.driver as cuda
import numpy as np

runtime = trt.Runtime(trt.Logger(trt.Logger.WARNING))

with open("champion.plan", "rb") as f:
    champion_engine = runtime.deserialize_cuda_engine(f.read())
with open("challenger.plan", "rb") as f:
    challenger_engine = runtime.deserialize_cuda_engine(f.read())

champion_ctx = champion_engine.create_execution_context()
challenger_ctx = challenger_engine.create_execution_context()

stream_a = cuda.Stream()
stream_b = cuda.Stream()

def route(request_id: int, input_buf):
    is_canary = (hash(request_id) % 100) < 5
    ctx = challenger_ctx if is_canary else champion_ctx
    stream = stream_b if is_canary else stream_a
    ctx.execute_async_v2(bindings=input_buf, stream_handle=stream.handle)
    stream.synchronize()
    return is_canary

bytes.topic: Data Pipelines — feature engineering pipelines, data validation, drift detection
bytes.title: TensorRT Inference Pipelines — Validating Inputs and Detecting Data Drift Before Execution
bytes.body:
TensorRT engines are compiled for fixed input shapes and data types — feeding mismatched tensors silently produces garbage outputs or crashes at `context->enqueueV3()`. Build a lightweight validation layer that checks binding metadata against incoming data before every inference call.

Use `engine->getTensorDataType(name)` and `engine->getTensorShape(name)` to extract expected specs at startup, then compare against your runtime input tensors. For drift detection, maintain a rolling mean and variance of input feature distributions using Welford's online algorithm. If the z-score of a feature's current batch mean exceeds a threshold (e.g. 3σ), flag it before the tensor ever hits the engine.

This matters because TensorRT's optimization profiles (`IOptimizationProfile`) define min/opt/max shape ranges — inputs near the boundaries trigger re-selection of the closest profile, degrading latency. Catching this at the pipeline level lets you reroute or rescale before paying the profile-switch cost.

Store per-feature stats in a circular buffer on the host; the overhead is negligible compared to `cudaMemcpyAsync` and kernel launch latency.

bytes.topic: Infrastructure — GPU cluster management, spot instances for training, cost optimization
bytes.title: TensorRT on Spot Instances — Surviving Interruptions with ITimingCache
bytes.body:
TensorRT engine builds are expensive — calibration and kernel selection on large models can take 30–90 minutes. On spot instances, an interruption mid-build means starting over. The fix: persist both the serialized engine and the ITimingCache.

TensorRT's timing cache stores layer-specific kernel benchmarking results. On a resumed instance, config.set_timing_cache() skips re-benchmarking already-solved layers, cutting subsequent build time by 60–80%. Critically, timing caches are GPU-architecture-specific — an A100 cache is invalid on a T4. Tag your S3/blob storage objects with the CUDA compute capability (sm_86, sm_80, etc.) to avoid silent cache mismatches across heterogeneous fleets.

For multi-GPU clusters, build once on a representative node and broadcast the serialized engine. Engines built without BuilderFlag.VERSION_COMPATIBLE are not portable across TensorRT versions or GPU families — enforce homogeneous instance types in your spot fleet. Use IBuilderConfig.set_memory_pool_limit(WORKSPACE, ...) to tune per-GPU memory without triggering a full rebuild; workspace size doesn't invalidate the engine cache.

Enabling BuilderFlag.FP16 reduces engine size and inference latency on Ampere+ GPUs, directly lowering per-GPU cost without rerunning INT8 calibration.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorrt as trt, os

TRT_LOGGER = trt.Logger(trt.Logger.WARNING)

def build_with_cache(onnx_path, engine_path, timing_cache_path):
    builder = trt.Builder(TRT_LOGGER)
    config = builder.create_builder_config()
    config.set_memory_pool_limit(trt.MemoryPoolType.WORKSPACE, 2 << 30)
    config.set_flag(trt.BuilderFlag.FP16)

    cache_bytes = open(timing_cache_path, "rb").read() if os.path.exists(timing_cache_path) else b""
    timing_cache = config.create_timing_cache(cache_bytes)
    config.set_timing_cache(timing_cache, ignore_mismatch=False)

    network = builder.create_network(1 << int(trt.NetworkDefinitionCreationFlag.EXPLICIT_BATCH))
    trt.OnnxParser(network, TRT_LOGGER).parse_from_file(onnx_path)
    serialized = builder.build_serialized_network(network, config)
    open(engine_path, "wb").write(serialized)

    with timing_cache.serialize() as buf:
        open(timing_cache_path, "wb").write(bytes(buf))
